coredata.dc:description: Photothermal therapy (PTT) of tumor would ineluctably cause oxidative stress and related inflammation in adjacent normal tissues, leading to a discounted therapeutic outcome. To address this issue, herein an innovative therapeutic strategy that integrates photothermal anticancer and normal cell protection is developed. A new type of nitrogen-doped carbon dot (ET-CD) has been synthesized in one step by hydrothermal method using ellagic acid and L-tyrosine as reaction precursors. The as-prepared ET-CD exhibits high photothermal conversion efficiency and good photothermal stability. After intravenous injection, ET-CD can accumulate at the tumor site and the hyperthermia generated under near infrared laser irradiation effectively ablates tumor tissues, thereby significantly inhibiting tumor growth. Importantly, owing to the inherited antioxidant activity from ellagic acid, ET-CD can remove reactive oxygen and nitrogen species produced in the body and reduce the levels of inflammatory factors induced by oxidative stress, so as to alleviate the damage caused by heat-induced inflammation to normal cells and tissues while photothermal anticancer. These attractive features of ET-CD may open the exploration of innovative therapeutic strategies to promote the clinical application of PTT.

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: Schematic diagram of the preparation of ET-CD and its applications in photothermal anticancer therapy and anti-inflammation.

Characterization of ET-CD. (a) TEM (scale bar = 50 nm) and HR-TEM images (the inset, scale bar = 1 nm). (b) AFM image (scale bar = 140 nm) (the inset: three-dimensional image) and (c) corresponding height histogram of a selected CD. (d) UV–vis-NIR spectrum (25 μg/mL). (e) Optimum fluorescence excitation/emission spectra. The inset: optical photographs of the ET-CD suspension under daylight (left) and a 365-nm UV lamp (right). (f) FTIR spectrum. (g) XPS survey spectrum. (h) High-resolution C 1s spectra. (i) High-resolution N 1s spectra.

Photothermal performance characterization of ET-CD: (a) Vis-NIR absorption spectra of ET-CD solutions at different concentrations. (b) Photothermal heating curves of ET-CD solutions at different concentrations (808 nm, 1.0 W/cm2). (c) Photothermal heating curves of ET-CD solutions (100 μg/mL) at different laser power densities. (d) Infrared thermal images of water and an ET-CD suspension (100 μg/mL). (e) Cyclic heating curves of five laser on/off cycles. (f) Heating-cooling curves of a single laser on/off process. (g) Fitting curve of cooling time versus negative natural logarithm of driving force temperature obtained from the cooling stage data of (f).

RONS scavenging activities of ET-CD. (a) Absorption spectra of ABTS + solutions in the presence of ET-CD with different concentrations and (b) corresponding elimination percentage of ABTS +. (c) Absorption spectra of DPPH solutions mixed with different concentrations of ET-CD and (d) corresponding clearance percentage of DPPH. (e) Absorption spectra of SA after reaction with OH radicals in the presence of different concentrations of ET-CD and (f) corresponding scavenging percentage of OH. (g) Percentage of O2
− elimination by different concentrations of ET-CD. (h) Illustration of RONS scavenging by ET-CD.

Photothermal experiments of ET-CD at the cellular level. (a) Photothermal heating curves of 4T1 cells incubated with different concentrations of ET-CD (808 nm, 1 W/cm2). (b) Infrared thermal images (808 nm, 1 W/cm2, 10 min) of 4T1 cells incubated with different concentrations of ET-CD. (c) Cell viability of 4T1 cells after incubation with ET-CD (100 μg/mL) and subsequent laser irradiation (808 nm, 8 min) with different power densities. (d) Cell viability of 4T1 cells incubated with different concentrations of ET-CD after laser irradiation for different duration. (e) CLSM images of 4T1 cells co-stained with Calcein-AM/PI probes after different treatment. Scale bar: 200 μm.

Cell viability of HUVECs after co-incubation with different concentrations of (a) H2O2 and (b) LPS without or with ET-CD (100 μg/mL) pretreatment. (c) CLSM images of HUVECs co-stained with Calcein-AM/PI probes after oxidative stress induced by H2O2. Scale bar: 200 μm. (d) Apoptosis analysis by flow cytometry of HUVECs exposed to different treatments. (e) CLSM images to assess RONS clearance activity of ET-CD on H2O2-treated HUVECs using DCFH-DA, DHE, DAF-FM DA, and DHR 123 as the detection probes. Scale bar: 200 μm.

(a) NIR photothermal images of mice exposed to NIR laser irradiation at 6 h after injection of ET-CD and (b) corresponding time-dependent photothermal heating curves of tumor sites. (c) Schematic diagram of in vivo tumor treatment procedure. (d) Relative tumor volume change of 4 groups. (e) Average tumor weight of different groups at day 20 and (f) the corresponding tumor photograph.
(a) H&E, TUNEL, and Ki-67 staining of tumor sections in different groups. Scale bar: 100 μm. (b) TNF-α and (c) IL-6 levels in serum of different tumor-bearing groups.
Expression levels of (a) TNF-α, (b) IL-6, and (c) IL-1β factors in the livers of various groups measured by ELISA.

head.ce:title: Nitrogen-doped carbon dots derived from ellagic acid and L-tyrosine for photothermal anticancer and anti-inflammation

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Photothermal therapy (PTT) of tumor would ineluctably cause oxidative stress and related inflammation in adjacent normal tissues, leading to a discounted therapeutic outcome. To address this issue, herein an innovative therapeutic strategy that integrates photothermal anticancer and normal cell protection is developed. A new type of nitrogen-doped carbon dot (ET-CD) has been synthesized in one step by hydrothermal method using ellagic acid and L-tyrosine as reaction precursors. The as-prepared ET-CD exhibits high photothermal conversion efficiency and good photothermal stability. After intravenous injection, ET-CD can accumulate at the tumor site and the hyperthermia generated under near infrared laser irradiation effectively ablates tumor tissues, thereby significantly inhibiting tumor growth. Importantly, owing to the inherited antioxidant activity from ellagic acid, ET-CD can remove reactive oxygen and nitrogen species produced in the body and reduce the levels of inflammatory factors induced by oxidative stress, so as to alleviate the damage caused by heat-induced inflammation to normal cells and tissues while photothermal anticancer. These attractive features of ET-CD may open the exploration of innovative therapeutic strategies to promote the clinical application of PTT.

ce:data-availability.ce:para: Data will be made available on request.

ce:section.ce:para: Photothermal therapy (PTT) is an emerging tumor treatment modality, in which the photothermal agents accumulated in the tumor site convert the absorbed exogenous near infrared (NIR) light into heat to ablate tumor tissues. PTT has attracted great interest in cancer treatment due to its non-invasiveness, high efficiency, and therapeutic specificity [1,2]. In recent years, many nanomaterials with high levels of photothermal conversion efficiency have been explored for PTT applications, such as noble metal nanostructures [3,4], carbon-based nanomaterials [5–7], transition metal chalcogenides [8,9], and metal oxides [10,11]. Although PTT has unique advantages in anti-tumor, the inevitable thermal diffusion in the treatment process is prone to cause the oxidative stress response of adjacent normal cells and tissues, which would lead to rapid rise of inflammatory factors and accelerates the establishment of inflammatory microenvironment [12,13]. These adverse factors will hinder the clinical application of PTT in cancer treatment. Therefore, it is urgent to develop innovative therapeutic strategies that integrate photothermal anticancer and normal cell protection.

In the past few decades, numerous studies have shown that there is a close link between cancer development and inflammation [14–16]. The inflammatory microenvironment provides the potential conditions for cancer cell invasion and metastasis. Therefore, the prevention of inflammation outbreaks must be considered while exploring PTT-based cancer treatment strategies. Based on these considerations, some relevant explorations have been carried out. Tang et al. has combined polydopamine nanospheres with aspirin to develop a multifunctional nanoagent that can effectively eliminate the inflammatory reaction during photothermal anticancer [17]. Mao et al. have developed a therapeutic nanodrug (Qu-FeIIP) based on the coordination of quercetin and metal ions, which can achieve PTT ablation of tumors and avoid heat stress to normal tissues [12]. Molybdenum-based polyoxometalate clusters have also been constructed for photothermal anti-tumor and ROS clearance utilizing the charge transfer between Mo (VI) and Mo (V) states [13]. The combination strategy of organic microchromophore-based photothermal nanoagents and non-steroidal anti-inflammatory drugs can alleviate the inflammation and immunosuppression in tumor microenvironment, thereby enhancing the efficacy of anti-tumor therapy [18]. Despite the interesting results achieved, these nano-therapeutic agents still have some shortcomings, such as the multi-step cumbersome preparation process and/or the unavoidable higher toxicity of metal-containing materials. There is still an urgent need to explore and develop novel PTT nanoagents with a simple preparation process and negligible toxic side effects for synergistic anti-tumor therapy and anti-inflammatory.

As emerging carbon-based nanomaterials with sizes below 10 nm, carbon dots (CDs) exhibit various fascinating features, including unique optical properties, hydrophilic nature, high stability, biological safety, and low cost, which make them promising in biomedical fields, such as drug delivery, biological imaging, and cancer treatment [19,20]. Generally, there are two main approaches to synthesize CDs, namely top-down and bottom-up [19,21,22]. Bottom-up synthesis is more preferred due to numerous molecular precursors available, large-scale synthesis, and more uniform properties of the prepared CDs [23,24]. Through reasonable precursor selection and synthetic strategy, the prepared CDs can be given with high NIR absorption capacity and outstanding photothermal conversion performance, and thus become promising alternative photothermal agents for tumor therapy [25–27]. In addition, the good tumor penetration conferred by the ultra-small size is conducive to improving the photothermal anticancer efficiency of CDs [28]. However, the photothermal properties alone are still not enough for CDs used in PTT. It is desirable that they should also possess normal cytoprotection and anti-inflammatory abilities. Fortunately, it has been found that certain structural features of the precursors can be retained in the generated CDs, which makes them inherit some of the original characteristics of the reactants [29,30]. In view of this, multifunctional CDs with intrinsic photothermal property and anti-inflammatory ability can be prepared by appropriate precursor combinations and optimized reaction conditions.

In this study, a new kind of nitrogen-doped CD (designated as ET-CD) with both photothermal and anti-inflammatory properties was prepared by high-temperature hydrothermal synthesis using ellagic acid (EA) and L-tyrosine (Tyr) as precursors (Fig. 1 ). EA is a natural polyphenolic organic substance with antioxidant properties that can scavenge various free radicals and inhibit lipid peroxidation, thus protecting biological molecules in the body from oxidation and nitrification damage by reactive oxygen nitrogen species (RONS) [31,32]. EA also has a significant inhibitory effect on a variety of carcinogenesis induced by chemical substances [33]. Tyr belongs to aromatic amino acids that can help form a larger conjugation structure in the process of carbonization while introducing nitrogen doping [34]. Studies have shown that nitrogen doping can endow the produced CD with better photoluminescence and higher photothermal conversion efficiency [35,36]. After basic characterization of the material morphology and structure, the photothermal conversion performance and RONS scavenging activity of ET-CD suspensions were systematically studied. Subsequently, a series of in vitro cell experiments were carried out to evaluate the performances of ET-CD in photothermal killing of tumor cells and protection of normal cells. Finally, relevant mouse models were established to study the application of ET-CD in photothermal ablation of tumors and anti-inflammatory in vivo. Positive results of in vitro and in vivo studies have shown that ET-CD can effectively ablate tumors while protecting normal tissue from inflammatory damage, demonstrating its advantages in improving the efficacy of photothermal tumor therapy.

EA, Tyr, salicylic acid (SA), lipopolysaccharides (LPS), and potassium persulfate (K2S2O8) were purchased from Shanghai Titan Technology Co., Ltd. Hydrogen peroxide (H2O2, 31 %) was acquired from Sinopharm Chemical Reagent Co., Ltd. 1,1-Diphenyl-2-picrylhydrazine (DPPH), 2,2′-azinobis (3-ethylbenzothiazoline-6-sulfonic acid) diammonium salt (ABTS), dihydroethidium (DHE, 95 %), and dihydrogen-rhodamine 123 (DHR123) were provided by Sigma-Aldrich, total superoxide dismutase (SOD) activity detection kit (NBT method), 4-amino-5-methylamino-2′,7′-difluorofluorescein diacetate (DAF-FM DA), calcein acetoxymethyl ester (Calcein-AM) /propidium iodide (PI), and 2′,7′-dichlorodihydrofluorescein diacetate (DCFH-DA) was purchased from Beyotime. All chemical reagents were used directly after receipt without any further purification. All aqueous solutions were prepared with deionized water (18.2 MΩ cm).

Transmission electron microscopy (TEM) and high-resolution TEM (HRTEM) images were obtained from a JEOL JEM-2199 microscope. Morphological analyses were performed by atomic force microscopy (AFM) with a Bruker Dimension Icon instrument. Fourier transform infrared (FT-IR) spectra were recorded on a Nicolet Avatar 370 FTIR spectrophotometer. X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS) spectra were measured by a Thermo Scientific K-Alpha apparatus. Zeta potential was measured by a Nano-ZS 90 analyzer. Ultraviolet-visible-NIR (UV–Vis-NIR) spectra were obtained on a BeckMan Coulter DU 730 spectrophotometer. Infrared photothermal data were collected using a FLIR A300 instrument. Fluorescence spectra were acquired with a Cary Eclipse fluorescence spectrophotometer. The absolute fluorescence quantum yield of ET-CD was measured with a quantum efficiency measurement system (Hamamatsu Photonics, C13534). Raman spectra were measured by a Jobin Yvon Raman system.

Briefly, 300 mg of EA and 730 mg of Tyr were added to 30 mL of sodium hydroxide solution (0.05 % w/v), and the mixture was exposed to ultrasonic treatment to obtain a white emulsion. The emulsion was then transferred to a stainless steel reactor lined with polytetrafluoroethylene (100 mL) and heated at 200 °C for 10 h. After cooling, the obtained brownish black solution was centrifuged (8000 rpm) and the supernatant was filtered by a filter membrane with a pore size of 0.22 μm to remove the large particles. The filtrate was then dialyzed in deionized water for 48 h using a dialysis bag with a molecular weight cutoff of 3500 Da. The dialyzed solution was freeze-dried to obtain the black powder for characterization.

To investigate the photothermal properties of ET-CD, different concentrations of ET-CD solutions were placed in transparent quartz cuvettes with lid, and each solution was irradiated with a NIR laser (808 nm, 1.0 W/cm2) for 10 min. The thermal imaging pictures and temperature changes of the solutions were recorded using an infrared thermal imager. ET-CD suspensions with fixed concentration of 100 μg/mL were selected to explore the heating curves of ET-CD under different laser power densities. For photothermal stability assessment, a material solution of 100 μg/mL was irradiated with the NIR laser (1.0 W/cm2) for 10 min and then naturally cooled for 10 min. The process was repeated 5 times to obtain heating-cooling cycle curves.

The photothermal conversion efficiency of ET-CD is calculated according to one photothermal heating-cooling curve of the material with a concentration of 100 μg/mL using the following equation [37]:

where η (%) is photothermal conversion efficiency, Tmax ₋ Tsurr (K) is the difference between the equilibrium temperature and the ambient temperature, Qdis (mW) is heat loss of the light absorbed by the container, I (W/cm2) is laser power density, A808 is the absorbance of the sample at 808 nm, h (W cm−2 K−1) represents heat transfer coefficient, s (cm2) represents the surface area of the container.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Detection of free radical scavenging capacity
ce:section.ce:para: The scavenging ability of ET-CD on ABTS free radicals was measured to evaluate its antioxidant capacity. An aqueous solution of ABTS (7.4 mM) was mixed with the K2S2O8 aqueous solution (2.6 mM) in equal volume. After reacting in the dark for 12 h, the mixture was diluted 40–50 times to obtain ABTS working solution. Subsequently, 2 mL of ABTS working solution was added to each ET-CD solution (0.5 mL) (concentrations of 0, 5, 20, 40, 60, 80, 100, and 120 μg/mL), and then the absorption spectra of the reaction solutions in the range of 400–1000 nm were recorded after 30 min of reaction in the dark. The scavenging rates of ET-CD on ABTA free radicals were calculated according to the formula: Elimination (%) = (A0 − Ax) / A0. A0 and Ax represent the absorbance of ABTS radicals at 734 nm in the absence and presence of ET-CD, respectively.

ce:section.ce:section-title: DPPH free radical scavenging activity of ET-CD
ce:section.ce:para: The scavenging efficacy of ET-CD on reactive nitrogen species (RNS) was evaluated by the DPPH assay. 2 mg of DPPH was dissolved in 50 mL of ethanol to obtain 100 μM DPPH working solution. 1 mL of DPPH working solution was mixed with the solutions of ET-CD. The total volume of each mixed solution was 2 mL, and the final concentrations of the material were 0, 5, 20, 40, 60, 80, 100, and 120 μg/mL. The mixed solutions were allowed to react in the dark for 30 min, and then their absorption spectra in the range of 400 to 800 nm were measured. Based on the absorbance of DPPH radicals at 517 nm, the clearance rates of DPPH radicals by ET-CD were calculated according to the formula: Elimination (%) = (A0 − Ax) / A0, where A0 and Ax were the absorbance of DPPH radicals at 517 nm in the absence and presence of ET-CD, respectively.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Clearance activity of hydroxyl radicals (OH) by ET-CD
ce:section.ce:para: SA was selected as an indicator to evaluate the eliminating ability of ET-CD on •OH radicals. First, aqueous solutions of ferrous sulfate (2 mM), SA (2 mM), and H2O2 (5 mM) were prepared respectively. Equal amount of ferrous ion solution (0.2 mL) and SA solution (0.2 mL) were mixed with a certain amount of material solution (Total volume: 3 mL; final concentrations of ET-CD: 0, 5, 20, 40, 60, 80, 100, and 120 μg/mL), and then the H2O2 solution (0.2 mL) was added to start the reaction. The absorption spectra of the reaction solutions in the range of 400–800 nm were collected with a UV–vis spectrophotometer at 15 min after reaction. The absorbance at 510 nm was selected to calculate the OH clearance rate of ET-CD using the formula similar to that for the above free radical eliminating assessment.

The total SOD activity detection kit (NBT method) was used to detect the SOD-like activity of ET-CD. According to the instructions of the kit, xanthine and xanthine oxidase were utilized to produce superoxide anions (O2
−), which reacted with nitro-blue tetrazolium (NBT) to produce blue formazan. The SOD-like activity of ET-CD was determined based on the absorbance drop of blue formazan at 560 nm in the presence of the material with different concentrations.

Mouse 4T1 breast cancer cells and human umbilical vein endothelial cells (HUVECs) were provided by Fudan University Shanghai Cancer Center. Both cell lines were cultured with Dulbecco's modified Eagle medium (DMEM) high-glucose medium containing 10 % fetal bovine serum (FBS). Cell passage was performed using 0.25 % trypsin containing ethylene diamine tetraacetic acid (0.02 %). For cell internalization investigation, ET-CD was first labeled with rose bengal (RB). 4T1 cells and HUVECs were incubated with the resulting ET-CD@RB for a certain time (2, 4, 6, and 8 h), respectively. After removing the excess material, the cells were stained with 4,6-diamino-2-phenyl indole (DAPI). Uptake of ET-CD@RB by the cells was detected by confocal laser scanning microscopy (CLSM) to determine the optimal uptake time of the material by 4 T1 cells and HUVECs (RB: λex = 543 nm, λem = 570–650 nm; DAPI: λex = 405 nm, λem = 420–510 nm).

HUVEC and 4T1 cells were inoculated on 96 well plates (1 × 104 cells per well) and cultured for 12 h. After that, different concentrations of ET-CD suspensions (0, 25, 50, 100, 200, and 400 μg/mL) were added and co-incubated with the cells for 12 and 24 h. Finally, the cell viabilities were measured by methyl thiazolyl tetrazolium (MTT) assays using a Thermo Scientific Multiskan MK3 enzyme mark instrument to evaluate the cytotoxicity of ET-CD.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Evaluation of the efficacy of in vitro PTT
ce:section.ce:para: In vitro photothermal therapeutic efficacy of ET-CD was assessed by the MTT method and Calcein-AM/PI staining. 4T1 cells were cultured in 96-well plates (1 × 104 cells per well) overnight, and then the original medium in the wells was replaced with the fresh medium containing different concentrations of ET-CD. After co-incubation for 6 h, excess ET-CD was removed and fresh medium was added. The cells were then illuminated with an 808-nm laser (1.0 W/cm2) for 8 min. The cell viabilities were measured by MTT assay. Photothermal imaging of the cells was also performed when the ET-CD-treated cells were exposed to laser irradiation using the infrared thermal imager. The effect of laser power density on in vitro PTT was also explored. The material concentration was fixed at 100 μg/mL, and other experimental steps were similar to those above.

In addition, four groups of (1) blank control, (2) only laser irradiation, (3) only ET-CD, and (4) ET-CD + laser irradiation were set up for Calien-AM/PI staining. 4T1 cells were seeded in CLSM special culture dishes and cultured overnight in the incubator to make them completely adherent. After the original culture medium was discarded, fresh DMEM medium without serum was added to groups (1) and (2), and fresh medium containing ET-CD (100 μg/mL) was added to groups (3) and (4). Following further incubation for 6 h and removal of excess material, groups (2) and (4) were illuminated with the laser (808 nm, 1 W cm−2) for 8 min. Finally, the 4 groups of cells were co-stained with Calcein-AM/PI (30 min) for CLSM observation (calcein-AM: excited by a 488-nm laser; PI: excited by a 543-nm laser) to analyze the killing effect of different treatments on 4T1 cells.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Evaluation of antioxidant efficacy in vitro
ce:section.ce:para: The antioxidant effect of ET-CD at the cell level was explored using HUVECs as a cell model. Different concentrations of H2O2 or LPS solutions were used to induce oxidative stress in HUVECs, producing RONS. Four groups were involved in the experiments: (1) Control group, (2) H2O2 (or LPS) group, (3) ET-CD group, and (4) H2O2 (or LPS) + ET-CD group. HUVECs seeded in 96-well plates were incubated with ET-CD (100 μg/mL) for 6 h. After removing excess material, the culture medium containing different concentrations of H2O2 (0.5, 1.0, 2.0, and 3.0 mM) or LPS (50, 100, 150, and 200 ng/mL) was added to the corresponding wells and the cells were incubated for another 2 h. Finally, the cell viability of each group was detected by MTT assay. In addition, HUVECs in the groups of (1) Control, (2) 2 mM H2O2 (or 200 ng/mL LPS), (3) ET-CD (100 μg/mL), and (4) 2 mM H2O2 (or 200 ng/mL LPS) + ET-CD (100 μg/mL) were co-stained with Calien-AM/PI to observe the protective effect of ET-CD on normal cells.

CLSM observation was further conducted to explore the effect of ET-CD on the removal of intracellular RONS induced by H2O2 or LPS. The experiments included four groups: (1) blank control group, (2) inducer group, (3) ET-CD group, and (4) inducer + ET-CD group. HUVECs were seeded in CLSM-special cell culture dishes and cultured overnight. After the cells were attached, the medium was aspirated and fresh DMEM medium without serum was added to groups (1) and (2). Medium containing ET-CD (100 μg/mL) was added to groups (3) and (4). After 6 h of co-incubation, the medium in groups (2) and (4) was replaced with a DMEM solution of H2O2 (2 mM) or LPS (200 ng/mL) and continued to incubate for 2 h. Subsequently, the cells in each group were stained with DCFH-DA (10 μM, 1.0 mL) for 15 min. Finally, the fluorescence of the generated 2′,7′-dichlorofluorescein (DCF) in the cells was observed by CLSM (λex: 488 nm) to analyze the RONS scavenging efficiency of ET-CD. Similar to the above procedure, the DCFH-DA indicator was replaced with DHE, DAF-FM DA, and DHR123 to detect the clearance of intracellular O2·− (red fluorescence), NO (green fluorescence) and ONOO− (green fluorescence), respectively. In addition, the effect of ET-CD in alleviating H2O2-induced apoptosis was also evaluated by flow cytometry.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Establishment of a 4T1 tumor-bearing mouse model
ce:section.ce:para: 5-week-old Balb/C female mice were obtained from Hangzhou Legen Biological Co., Ltd. All animal experiments were approved by the Experimental Animal Management and Animal Welfare Ethics Committee of Shanghai Normal University, and were carried out in strict accordance with the national and local Guidelines on the Care and Use of Laboratory Animals (China). To establish the tumor model, a PBS suspension of 4 T1 cells (5 × 106 cells, 200 μL) was injected subcutaneously into the right hind limb of each mouse. When the tumor volume reached about 100 mm3, in vivo photothermal imaging and tumor treatment could be performed.

ce:section.ce:section-title: In vivo blood circulation half-life and biodistribution measurements
ce:section.ce:para: To facilitate the measurement of circulatory half-life and biodistribution, ET-CD was labeled with 125I. Na125I (1 mCi) and ET-CD (200 μL, 1 mg/mL, in PBS) were transferred to an eppendorf centrifuge tube coated with Iodogen (100 μg) and oscillated at room temperature for 30 min. The 125I-labeled ET-CD was washed with PBS three times and the unlabeled 125I was removed by ultrafiltration.

For blood circulation half-life evaluation, 125I-labeled ET-CD dispersed in sterile saline (0.2 mL, 40 μCi injection/mouse, n = 3) was injected into the 4T1 tumor-bearing mice via tail vein. After intravenous injection, blood was drawn from the tail veins of mice at different time points using capillary tubes. Each blood sample was transferred to a pre-weighed radioimmunogamma counter tube and the radioactivity was counted for 15 s in a gamma radioimmunocounter (SN6110, Shanghai Nucleus Research Institute Rihuan Photoelectric Instrument Co., Ltd.) calibrated against a known aliquot of Na125I. After the measurement, the total weight of the tube and blood was weighed to obtain the weight of the blood samples. Data points were fit to an exponential decay function to determine the blood circulation half-lives.

For biodistribution measurement, tumor-bearing mice (three mice at each time point) were euthanized and the tissue samples (heart, liver, spleen, lung, kidney, and tumor) were collected at different time points after intravenous injection of 125I-labeled ET-CD (0.2 mL, 40 μCi injection/mouse, n = 3). After washing with PBS three times and sucking up the residual PBS with filter paper, all tissue samples were weighed and counted for 15 s with the gamma radioimmunocounter. Data for the tissue distribution were calculated using the percentage of injected dose per gram tissue (% ID/g).

ce:section.ce:section-title: Evaluation of PTT efficacy in vivo
ce:section.ce:para: 4 T1 tumor-bearing mice were randomly divided into the following four groups (n = 7): (1) PBS (Control), (2) PBS + Laser, (3) ET-CD, and (4) ET-CD + Laser. A PBS suspension of ET-CD was intravenously injected into the mice via tail vein (5 mg/mL, 200 μL). At 6 h after the injection, the tumors of groups (2) and (4) were exposed to laser irradiation (808 nm, 1.0 W/cm2) for 10 min. On the second day after the end of laser irradiation, tumors of one randomly selected mouse from each group were dissected for hematoxylin–eosin (H&E), terminal deoxynucleotidyl transferase-mediated deoxyuridine triphosphate-biotin nick end labeling (TUNEL), and Ki-67 staining. In addition, the blood was collected from three mice in each group, and the serum was extracted by centrifugation to determine the levels of tumor necrosis factor α (TNF-α) and interleukin-6 (IL-6) factors in serum. Tumor volume ((length × width2) / 2) and mouse weight were measured every two days during the follow-up monitoring, and the mice were photographed on days 0, 4, 8, 12, 16, and 20. At the end of 20 days, the mice in the 4 groups (4 mice in each group) were sacrificed, and the tumors were dissected, weighed, and photographed. One mouse in each group was randomly selected to collect the main organs (heart, liver, spleen, lung, and kidney) for H&E staining. At the same time, the blood of each group was collected for hematological index and blood biochemical analysis.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Evaluation of in vivo antioxidant therapy
ce:section.ce:para: An animal model of oxidative stress was established to explore the antioxidant activity of ET-CD in vivo. 4T1 tumor-bearing mice were randomly divided into the following 4 groups (n = 5): (1) Control, (2) H2O2, (3) ET-CD, and (4) ET-CD + H2O2. The mice in the first group were injected intravenously with PBS solution, and then the liver tissues were dissected out 12 h after the injection. The second group of mice received intravenous injection of H2O2 solution (10 mM) 2 h after PBS injection, and then the liver tissues were removed 10 h later. The mice in the third group were injected with PBS suspension of ET-CD via tail vein (5 mg/mL, 200 μL), followed by the dissection to collect the liver tissues 12 h after injection. In the last group, the mice were injected with ET-CD solution first, followed by injection of H2O2 solution (10 mM) 2 h after ET-CD injection, and the liver tissues were collected 10 h later. The fresh liver tissues were ground and centrifuged to prepare tissue homogenate. The expression of TNF-α, IL-6, and interleukin-1β (IL-1β) was measured by enzyme-linked immunosorbent assay (ELISA).

A two-tailed unpaired student's t-test has been utilized for statistical analysis. Data are represented as mean ± standard deviation. The “ns” indicates no significant difference. *p < 0.05 is regarded as statistically significant. **p < 0.01, ***p < 0.001 and ****p < 0.0001 are treated as extremely significant.

ET-CD was prepared by hydrothermal synthesis using EA and Tyr as precursors. According to TEM images (Fig. 2a) and particle size statistics (Fig. S1), the prepared ET-CD has a typical zero-dimensional structure with an average size of about 3.2 nm. Clear lattice stripes of a typical ET-CD with a lattice spacing of 0.21 nm can be observed by the HRTEM, which can be ascribed to the (100) crystal plane of CD [38]. The AFM results (Fig. 2b, c) indicate that the height distribution of ET-CD is around 1.9 nm. By contrast, the electron microscopic images showed that the sizes of CD synthesized from EA (E-CD) and the carbon nanosphere prepared from Tyr (T-CS) are about 13 nm and 200 nm, respectively (Fig. S2 a-d). The ET-CD suspension appears dark brown, which is significantly different from that of E-CD or T-CS (Fig. S3a). The X-ray diffraction (XRD) pattern of ET-CD shows a broad peak centered at 2θ = 26o (Fig. S3b), which corresponds to the (002) plane of the graphite-filled carbon core [39], and the (100) diffraction of graphitic carbon is also clearly observed. The UV–vis-NIR absorption spectrum of ET-CD indicates a gradually decrease trend from ultraviolet to NIR region (Fig. 2d). The absorption shoulder of ET-CD at around 230 nm can be attributed to the π-π* transition of the aromatic sp2 carbon core, while the bands at 280 and 330 nm correspond to the π-π*/n-π* transitions of the surface functional groups originating from the EA and Tyr precursors (Fig. S3c, d) [40,41]. Fig. 2e presents the fluorescence spectra of an ET-CD solution, which shows a wide emission peak centered at 430 nm under excitation at 330 nm. The emission peak position is basically independent of the excitation wavelength, but the fluorescence intensity changes remarkably (Fig. S4a-c). When illuminated by an ultraviolet lamp (365 nm), the ET-CD suspension exhibits bright blue fluorescence (the inset in Fig. 2e), while E-CD does not show visible fluorescence (Fig. S4d), so it can be inferred that the fluorescence emission of ET-CD mainly arises from the Tyr precursor. The absolute fluorescence quantum yield of ET-CD (100 μg/mL, λex = 330 nm) was measured to be ~3 %.

In the FTIR spectrum of ET-CD (Fig. 2f), the broad peak at 3650–3100 cm−1 corresponds to the stretching vibration of OH and NH components, and the peaks appearing at 2980–2830 cm−1 can be assigned to the CH vibrations. The strong absorption peaks at 1740–1550 cm−1 should be due to the overlap of CO and CC stretching vibrations. The IR transitions at 1514 and 1456 cm−1 are ascribed to the benzene skeletal vibrations, and the CH bending vibration peaks of the aromatic rings can be observed in the range of 880–600 cm−1. In addition, the absorption bands at 1385, 1248, 1173, and 1109 cm−1 can be indexed to the vibrations of CN, C-O-C, and CO [34,40]. It is clear that some of the characteristic infrared absorption peaks come from the associated groups of EA and Tyr precursors (Fig. S4e). The Raman spectrum of ET-CD exhibited typical D (sp3-hybrid) and G (sp2-hybrid) bands around 1360 cm−1 and 1580 cm−1, respectively, further demonstrating the formation of graphite structures (Fig. S4f) [42]. The intensity ratio of D and G bands (ID/IG) is about 1.06, indicating the existence of defects in ET-CD. XPS survey spectrum of ET-CD (Fig. 2g) shows the existence of C, N, and O elements, the contents of which are 74.32 %, 3.52 %, and 22.16 %, respectively. Specifically, high-resolution C1s XPS profiles exhibit four peaks at 284.6, 286.2, 288.4, and 291.1, which are assigned to C-C/C=C, CN, CO, and CO (Fig. 2h), respectively [42]. In the high-resolution N 1 s XPS spectra (Fig. 2i), the two peaks appearing at 400.1 and 402.1 eV belong to the amino nitrogen and graphite nitrogen, respectively [43]. In addition, the O1s spectra reveal the presence of CO, O=C-O, and C-O-H (Fig. S5) [34]. These characterization results indicate that the as-synthesized ET-CD still retain the relevant functional groups of the reaction precursors well after high-temperature carbonization, and it is inferred that the ET-CD has the potential of inheriting the intrinsic biological activities of its reaction precursors. Due to the abundant hydrophilic oxygen and nitrogen functional groups on the surface, ET-CD and the other two materials showed high negative Zeta potential values (Fig. S6a). ET-CD can be uniformly dispersed in PBS and DMEM +10 % FBS. The Zeta potential of ET-CD dispersions was measured for 10 days (Fig. S6b), and no significant change was found during the monitoring period, indicating that ET-CD has good dispersion stability in physiological solutions. This also reveals that ET-CD is not easily biodegraded at pH 7.4. However, when ET-CD was dispersed in PBS solution of pH 5.2, the degradation was observed after incubation for a period of time (Fig. S6c). Therefore, we speculate that ET-CD may be degraded in acidic physiological environments, such as lysosomes.

As shown in Fig. 3a, as the concentration of ET-CD increases, its NIR absorption is correspondingly enhanced, and the absorption value at 808 nm is linearly related to the material concentration. The strong NIR absorption gives ET-CD the possibility of NIR photothermal properties. In view of this, the temperature rise curves of ET-CD with different concentrations under NIR laser irradiation were measured. As expected, the ET-CD solutions exposed to 808-nm laser irradiation showed significant irradiation time- and concentration-dependent heating effects, while the control water exhibited negligible temperature change (Fig. 3b). After 10 min of laser irradiation, the temperature of solutions with concentrations of 200 and 400 μg/mL increased by 25.2 and 35.8 °C, respectively. In addition, it was found that the solution temperature increased with the increase of laser power density (Fig. 3c). The temperature rise of ET-CD under laser irradiation can be observed more directly by the infrared thermography of the material and control water (Fig. 3d). Therefore, the as-prepared ET-CD exhibits good photothermal heating properties under the irradiation of NIR laser and is expected to be applied in PTT to kill cancer cells.

For a photothermal agent, thermal stability and photothermal conversion efficiency play crucial roles in its performance. In view of this, the photothermal stability of ET-CD was explored by exposing an ET-CD suspension (100 μg/mL) to NIR laser irradiation for several consecutive laser on/off cycles. During 5 times of heating-cooling cycles, the material showed similar temperature variation in each cycle, thus proving that ET-CD has good photothermal stability to resist photobleaching (Fig. 3e). The photothermal conversion efficiency of ET-CD is calculated to be 36.8 % based on the heating-cooling curve of 100 μg/mL ET-CD (Fig. 3f,g) using the reported method [37,44]. This conversion efficiency is comparable to that of polydopamine CD (35 %) [45] and cyanine CD (38.7 %) [46], but remarkably higher than that reported for carbon quantum dots synthesized from urea and citric acid (24.3 %) [47] and graphitic pencil nanodots (27 %) [48].

By comparing the UV–vis-NIR absorption spectra of ET-CD, E-CD and T-CS (Fig. S7a), it can be observed that both ET-CD and E-CD have relatively strong absorption at 808 nm. However, the photothermal heating curves under the same conditions reveal that only ET-CD has the ability to effectively convert the absorbed NIR light into heat, while E-CD and T-CS do not show heating phenomenon with the extension of laser irradiation time (Fig. S7b-c). Based on these experimental results, it is speculated that EA and Tyr form large conjugated structures under high temperature and high pressure hydrothermal environment, which leads to a high degree of carbonization and makes the prepared ET-CD has good photothermal properties [34]. In addition, the doping of graphite-N can significantly reduce the HOMO-LUMO gap and the corresponding transition energy (Fig. S8) [34], which may also make an important contribution to the NIR photothermal performance of ET-CD.

In addition to the photothermal properties, the prepared ET-CD is expected to maintain the excellent antioxidant properties of EA, which is one of the raw materials for preparation of ET-CD. Two classical free radicals, namely ABTS + and DPPH, were utilized to evaluate the antioxidant capacity of ET-CD. The ABTS working solution was turquoise with a maximum absorption at 734 nm, which could be discolored by the radical-trapping antioxidants. As shown in Fig. 4a and b, the scavenging rate of ABTS + increased with increasing ET-CD concentration. When the ET-CD concentration was 120 μg/mL, the ABTS + clearance rate reached >80 % (Fig. S9a-c), suggesting that the prepared ET-CD has good scavenging activity against free radicals. DPPH is a very stable nitrogen-centered radical with a maximum absorption peak of about 515 nm in the visible region. When antioxidants are added, the unpaired electron in DPPH can be paired, resulting in a gradual fading of its dark purple color. In the DPPH clearance experiment, ET-CD also showed highly efficient RNS scavenging activity in a concentration-dependent manner (Fig. 4c and d, Fig. S9d-f), with about 80 % of DPPH eliminated at a material concentration as low as 120 μg/mL. The scavenging ability of ET-CD to ABTS + and DPPH radicals is only slightly lower than that of E-CD.

Among the common ROS, hydroxyl radical (OH) has the highest electrochemical potential (E(OH/H2O) = 2.80 V), so it is more destructive to normal cells than other ROS. Herein, the specific probe SA was used as an indicator of OH to explore the scavenging activity of ET-CD on OH. SA is able to react with OH to produce 2,3-dihydroxybenzoic acid, which has a distinct absorption peak at about 510 nm. The test results revealed that ET-CD exhibited good scavenging activity against OH that was produced by Fenton reaction (Fig. 4e and f), and its clearance ability on •OH was significantly better than that of E-CD and T-CS at the same concentration of 120 μg/mL (Fig. S9g-i).

To further evaluate the eliminating activity of ET-CD against O2 − , the total SOD activity detection kit (NBT method) was used for detecting the SOD-like enzyme activity of ET-CD. The O2 − anions produced by the reaction system of xanthine and xanthine oxidase can reduce the nitroblue tetrazolium to blue formazan, which has strong absorption at 560 nm. SOD and SOD-like materials would remove O2 − anions, thus inhibiting the formation of formazan. In the presence of the nanozyme, the lighter the blue color of the reaction solution, the higher the SOD-like enzyme activity. Accordingly, the SOD-like activity level of ET-CD can be calculated by colorimetric analysis. As expected, the calculated results based on the absorbance at 560 nm (Fig. 4g) showed that the SOD-like activity of ET-CD was positively correlated with their concentration. At a low concentration of 120 μg/mL, the clearance rate of O2 − has reached >75 %. These results indicate that ET-CD has good SOD-like enzyme activity to effectively remove O2 − . As shown in Fig. 4h, we speculated that the antioxidant activity of ET-CD is mainly derived from the EA precursor, and the catechol type phenol hydroxyl groups on the surface of ET-CD can adsorb and convert RONS harmful to organisms into non-harmful substances, thus achieving the antioxidant effect [49,50].

ce:section.ce:section-title: Cellular uptake and cytotoxicity of ET-CD
ce:section.ce:para: 4T1 mouse breast cancer cell line is a cell model commonly used in breast cancer treatment research. In our case, 4T1 cells were utilized as a model to explore the inhibitory effect of photothermal treatment on tumor cells using ET-CD. Before exploring in vitro PTT, cellular uptake investigation of ET-CD is needed to determine the optimal incubation time point for laser irradiation. To facilitate the CLSM observation, ET-CD@RB was prepared by labeling ET-CD with RB. After co-incubation for 4 h, strong red fluorescence appeared in 4T1 cells (Fig. S10a-b). At 6 h, the fluorescence intensity reached the maximum, and then decreased with further extension of incubation time, indicating that the optimal uptake time of ET-CD in 4T1 cells was 6 h. There was a clear overlap between the red fluorescence of ET-CD@RB and the blue fluorescence of DAPI, suggesting that the material was able to enter the nucleus due to its small size. The optimal uptake time (6 h) of ET-CD by 4T1 cells was further verified by flow cytometry, as shown in the Fig. S10c. HUVECs were selected as the representative of normal cells to explore the protective effect of ET-CD on normal cells. The uptake of ET-CD@RB by HUVECs was also explored (Fig. S10d-e). The strongest red fluorescence signal could be observed when HUVECs were incubated with ET-CD@RB for 4–6 h. Therefore, the incubation time of ET-CD with the cells was selected as 6 h in the subsequent in vitro PTT and cell protection experiments.

Next, the cytotoxicity of ET-CD to 4T1 cells and HUVECs was assessed by MTT assay. At concentrations of ET-CD ranging from 0 to 400 μg/mL, the survival rate of 4T1 cells and HUVECs after incubation with the material for 12 and 24 h remained above 80 % (Fig. S11a, b), which indicated that ET-CD had very low cytotoxicity. In addition, blood compatibility is an important factor to ensure the safety of intravenous administration of nanotherapeutic agents. Accordingly, the hemolysis experiments were also carried out to assess the blood compatibility of ET-CD (Fig. S11c, d). The results showed that no obvious hemolysis occurred in the experimental concentration range of ET-CD. When the material concentration reached as high as 800 μg/mL, the hemolysis rate was still only about 2.5 %, indicating that there was no obvious erythrocyte damage. The low cytotoxicity and good blood compatibility of ET-CD are conducive to its further application in tumor therapy and normal cell protection.

The outstanding photothermal properties of ET-CD promoted further PTT experiments in vitro. In the beginning, 4T1 cells were inoculated into 96 wells, and the cells in each of the adjacent 4 wells were incubated with the same concentration of ET-CD. After co-incubation, the cells were exposed to NIR laser irradiation. As indicated in Fig. 5a, the cellular temperature increased with the prolongation of irradiation time and the increase of material concentration. Thermal imaging images visually show the effect of material concentration on temperature rise. At 400 μg/mL of material concentration, the cells were heated from 32.2 to 48.2 °C by 10 min of irradiation (Fig. 5b). Subsequently, the effect of photothermal action of the material on cell killing was evaluated by MTT assay. Under the conditions of a fixed material concentration (100 μg/mL) and different laser power density (808 nm, 8 min), the cell survival rate showed a decreasing trend with the increase of laser power density (Fig. 5c). At the laser power density of 2 W/cm2, the cell viability was reduced to only 20 %. Right after, the laser power density was fixed at 1 W/cm2 to explore the effects of laser irradiation time and material concentration on cell viability. As shown in Fig. 5d, the cell viability decreased significantly with the prolongation of irradiation time and the increase of material concentration. At a material concentration of 400 μg/mL, the cell survival rate was reduced to <20 % after 8 min of laser irradiation.

In addition, 4T1 cells after different treatment were co-stained with Calien-AM and PI to distinguish live and dead cells and then observed by CLSM for visually evaluating the therapeutic effect (Fig. 5e). Only a small amount of red fluorescence was detected in the laser irradiation group (808 nm, 1 W/cm2, 8 min), while almost no dead cells were found in the material (100 μg/mL) group, indicating that the laser irradiation or the material treatment alone had no obvious damage to the cells. In contrast, substantial red fluorescence could be clearly observed in the ET-CD + Laser treatment group, suggesting that most of the cells have been killed. The live/dead staining results are basically consistent with the MTT data. In addition, the photothermal killing ability of ET-CD on tumor cells was further verified by apoptosis analysis (Fig. S12). After incubation with ET-CD followed by laser irradiation, the tumor cells showed significantly increased apoptotic rates, with early apoptosis of 37.2 % and late apoptosis of 29.0 %, while the proportion of healthy cells was >91 % in the cells treated with laser irradiation or ET-CD alone. These positive results demonstrate that ET-CD has an efficient photothermal treatment effect at the cellular level, which provides the potential for further in vivo PTT studies using ET-CD. Although ET-CD synthesized from EA and Tyr has good photothermal properties, its photothermal conversion efficiency still needs to be enhanced. In subsequent studies, it is necessary to explore the selection of suitable precursors to significantly improve the photothermal properties of the prepared CDs, so that they can kill tumor cells with lower NIR power density while protecting normal cells.

ce:section.ce:section-title: In vitro antioxidant activity of ET-CD
ce:section.ce:para: Inspired by the high efficiency of ET-CD in removing RONS, in vitro antioxidant effects of this material were investigated using HUVECs as a model cell line. During the experiments, H2O2 or LPS was utilized to induce the generation of RONS in the cells. As shown by the results of MTT experiments (Fig. 6a-b), the survival rate of HUVECs decreased significantly with the increasing concentration of H2O2 or LPS, indicating that high concentration of H2O2 or LPS had obvious damage to the cells. The cells treated with ET-CD alone showed no significant decrease in cell survival due to negligible cytotoxicity of the material. After HUVECs were pre-treated with ET-CD and then exposed to H2O2 or LPS, the survival rate of the cells almost returned to the level of the blank control group, indicating that ET-CD could effectively inhibit the damage of H2O2 or LPS to normal cells. The protective effect of ET-CD on the normal cells was also judged by CLSM observation using live/dead staining. The live/dead staining results (Figs. 6c, S13a) showed that co-incubation of the cells with H2O2 or LPS alone would cause a large number of cell deaths, while most of the cells exposed to H2O2 or LPS could still survive after pretreatment with ET-CD, thus further demonstrating that ET-CD could effectively alleviate the cell death induced by H2O2 or LPS. In addition, the inhibitory effect of ET-CD against apoptosis induced by H2O2 was confirmed by flow cytometry (Fig. 6d). After H2O2 treatment, the percentage of healthy cells was reduced to only 56.2 %, and the early and late apoptotic rates were 17.9 % and 21.4 %, respectively. However, the apoptosis rate in the ET-CD + H2O2 group decreased to only 7.69 %, which was close to the normal control group.

To explore the protective mechanisms of ET-CD on normal cells, different groups of cells were stained with DCFH-DA. DCFH-DA is commonly used to detect ROS and nitric oxide (NO) as well as to determine the overall degree of cellular oxidative stress [51]. The HUVECs treated with only H2O2 (2 mM) or LPS (200 ng/mL) showed strong green fluorescence that indicated high oxidative stress, while no fluorescence emerged in the control and ET-CD groups. Excitingly, only very weak fluorescence was observed in the ET-CD + H2O2 (or ET-CD + LPS) group, indicating that ET-CD could effectively remove RONS in cells induced by H2O2 or LPS and alleviate cellular oxidative stress (Fig. 6e, S13b). Considering that O2
− anions can further react with nitrogen radicals such as NO to generate highly oxidizing peroxynitrite anions (ONOO−), the scavenging ability of ET-CD on O2 − , NO, and ONOO− was further explored. DHE, DAF-FM, and DHR123 were employed as fluorescent probes to detect intracellular O2 − (red fluorescence), NO (green fluorescence), and ONOO− (green fluorescence) induced by H2O2 or LPS, respectively (Figs. 6e, S13b). CLSM observation revealed that the cells treated with H2O2 or LPS exhibited significant fluorescence signals after staining with each of the three probes, implying the production of large amounts of these three active substances. In sharp contrast, only extremely weak fluorescence signals were observed in the ET-CD + H2O2 and ET-CD + LPS groups. These experimental results demonstrate that the powerful antioxidant capability of ET-CD enables it to efficiently remove RONS substances in the cells, thereby alleviating the cell damage caused by various oxidative stress. The outstanding cytoprotective effect of ET-CD makes it has a strong potential for anti-inflammatory applications.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Blood circulation half-lives and biodistribution in vivo of ET-CD
ce:section.ce:para: To facilitate the study of its behavior in vivo, ET-CD was labeled with 125I, a clinically commonly used radionuclide. The 125I-labeled ET-CD (40 μCi injection/mouse) was intravenously injected into the tumor bearing mice, and then the blood samples were drawn at different time points post injection for radioactive signal analysis (Fig. S14a). The blood-circulation curve of 125I-labeled ET-CD followed a two-compartment model, and the first-phase half-life and second-phase half-life were calculated to be 7.9 and 141.4 min, respectively.

Next, the time-dependent bio-distribution of 125I-labeled ET-CD in the main organs and tumors was also studied. As indicated in Fig. S14b, ET-CD was mainly taken up by the liver and kidneys, and its accumulation in these two organs reached a maximum of ~31.77 and 19.99 % ID/g, respectively, 6 h after injection. Subsequently, the content of ET-CD in each organ gradually decreased, indicating that ET-CD can be eliminated from the organs. Due to its small size, ET-CD can be excreted through urine via renal metabolism. The bio-distribution data also revealed that ET-CD could accumulate at the tumor site, and the amount of ET-CD accumulation in the tumor reached the maximum (6.34 % ID/g) at 6 h after injection. Considering that nanoparticles smaller than 10 nm are easily cleared by the kidneys, in subsequent related studies, it is better to design and prepare CDs of appropriate particle size (10–50 nm) to ensure that they have a longer blood half-life and a higher tumor enrichment rate.

ce:section.ce:section-title: In vivo photothermal anticancer activity of ET-CD
ce:section.ce:para: Prior to PTT investigation on the 4T1 mouse tumor model, photothermal imaging in vivo was performed to determine the optimal time point for NIR laser irradiation. Following intravenous injection of ET-CD, the tumor site was irradiated with NIR laser (808 nm, 1 W/cm2) every 3 h for 10 min, and meanwhile, the photothermal images of mice and the heating curves of the tumor site at different irradiation time points were obtained (Fig. S15a-b). Apparently, the injected ET-CD could efficiently accumulate in the tumor and maintain good photothermal conversion performance at the tumor site. Under laser irradiation conducted at 6 h after injection, the tumor site showed the most significant temperature rise, reaching about 17 °C, which was enough to kill tumor cells, while the tumor temperature change of the mice injected with PBS was only about 2.5 °C (Fig. 7a-b). Photothermal imaging results indicate that the optimal enrichment time of ET-CD in the mice is 6 h, which is consistent with the biological distribution of 125I labeled ET-CD. Therefore, 6 h after injection was selected as the optimal time point for in vivo PTT using ET-CD.

Motivated by satisfactory in vitro PTT and in vivo photothermal imaging, the in vivo PTT efficiency of ET-CD was evaluated using the 4 T1 tumor model (Fig. 7c). The 4T1 tumor-bearing mice were randomly divided into 4 groups. ET-CD suspensions were intravenously injected into the mice, and then the tumors were exposed to NIR laser irradiation (808 nm, 1 W/cm2, 8 min) at 6 h after injection. From material injection and subsequent laser irradiation to the end of the follow-up, the entire cycle lasted 20 days, during which the tumor volume was measured every two days. The resulting curves of relative tumor volume change are shown in Fig. 7d. Among the three groups of PBS, PBS + Laser, and ET-CD, the tumors showed a continuous growth trend, and there was no significant difference in the weight and size of ex vivo tumors on the last day (Fig. 7e-f), suggesting that laser irradiation and material injection alone had no obvious inhibitory effect on tumor growth. Inspiringly, only the mice underwent ET-CD administration and subsequent laser irradiation showed significant tumor ablation and inhibition (Figs. 7d-f, S16a). On the last day, the tumor suppression rate was as high as 92.4 %, and in one of the mice, the tumor had disappeared completely, indicating an outstanding in vivo photothermal treatment effect.

After laser irradiation, one mouse was randomly selected from each group, and the tumors were dissected for H&E, TUNEL, and Ki-67 staining to further explore the treatment effect. As shown in the H&E and TUNEL staining images (Fig. 8a), significant damage and cell apoptosis could be observed in the tumor tissue from ET-CD + Laser group, while no obvious abnormality was observed in other groups. Ki-67 staining analysis also showed that the proliferation of tumor cells in the ET-CD + Laser group was markedly inhibited compared with other groups. The results of histological examinations further demonstrate that the photothermal effect of ET-CD could efficiently ablate tumor tissue and inhibit tumor growth.

Meanwhile, the adjacent muscle tissue of the tumors was harvested for H&E, TUNEL, and Ki-67 staining as well as MDA level detection. As indicated in Fig. S17, the use of ET-CD for tumor PTT did not affect the normal cell activity of the adjacent muscle tissue, nor did it increase the concentration of intracellular ROS and the degree of lipid peroxidation in the surrounding normal tissue. By comparison, laser irradiation alone unavoidably caused some level of damage to adjacent normal tissue. It can be concluded that ET-CD can protect the surrounding normal tissue from the negative effects of PTT. Moreover, the blood of mice (n = 3) in each group was collected and centrifuged to obtain serum for detecting the expression of inflammatory cytokines. The levels of TNF-α and IL-6 factors were slightly higher in the Laser group compared with the PBS group (Fig. 8b-c), while the levels of these two factors in groups of ET-CD and ET-CD + Laser were decreased or similar to those in the PBS group, indicating that ET-CD could alleviate the pro-inflammatory response caused by PTT.

During 20 days of treatment and follow-up, the four groups of mice steadily gained weight (Fig. S16b) and showed no abnormal behaviors. After 20 days of monitoring, the blood of mice in each group was collected for routine blood and blood biochemical index analysis. In addition, the main organs of mice (heart, liver, spleen, lung, and kidney) were also harvested for H&E staining. Compared with the PBS group, the blood routine and blood biochemical indexes of the other three groups of mice were all in the normal range and did not show significant differences (Figs. S18, S19). Similarly, histopathological analysis results showed that there were no significant lesions and abnormalities in the main organ tissues of mice from each group (Fig. S20). Therefore, the ET-CD nanoagent has excellent biocompatibility and satisfactory therapeutic safety.

ce:section.ce:section-title: In vivo anti-oxidative stress capacity of ET-CD
ce:section.ce:para: It is known that H2O2 can induce macrophages to produce high levels of tumor necrosis factor-α (TNF-α), leading to severe inflammatory responses [52]. In view of this, a H2O2 solution (10 mM) was intravenously injected to induce oxidative stress response in the mice bearing 4T1 tumor, and the anti-inflammation activity of ET-CD was evaluated by measuring the levels of inflammatory factors in the liver. As shown in Fig. 9 a-c, H2O2 induced strong oxidative stress response in vivo, and the TNF-α, IL-6, and IL-1β levels in mouse liver tissue increased significantly. In contrast, these three indexes in the ET-CD and ET-CD + H2O2 groups were not significantly different from corresponding detection data in the PBS group. These results further demonstrate that ET-CD can effectively alleviate RONS-mediated inflammatory in vivo and protect normal organs and tissues in the living body from oxidative damage.

In summary, ET-CD with intrinsic photothermal performance and anti-inflammatory activity has been developed as an innovative therapeutic agent for cancer treatment and normal tissue protection. Owing to the high photothermal conversion efficiency and good photothermal stability, ET-CD enriched at the tumor site can thermally ablate tumor and effectively inhibit tumor growth under NIR laser irradiation. Importantly, the prepared ET-CD inherits the antioxidant activity of EA, one of its precursors, and thus is capable of effectively clearing RONS and down-regulating the levels of inflammatory factors. Therefore, ET-CD can alleviate the inflammatory damage caused by thermal ablation on normal tissues while implementing photothermal anticancer, realizing successful combination of PTT and inflammatory response suppression. Besides, ET-CD also shows negligible bio-toxicity and good biocompatibility. The comprehensive characteristics of ET-CD in PTT and anti-inflammation can reduce the negative effects of inflammation caused by photothermal anticancer, which provides a new strategy of “killing two birds with one stone” to promote the clinical transformation of PTT.

Xueli Ye: Writing – original draft, Methodology, Investigation, Data curation, Conceptualization. Zhonghuan Qu: Methodology, Investigation, Validation. Yuekai Wu: Validation, Methodology, Investigation. Shasha Zhao: Supervision, Funding acquisition. Juan Mou: Supervision, Methodology. Shiping Yang: Resources. Huixia Wu: Writing – review & editing, Supervision, Project administration, Funding acquisition, Conceptualization.

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
